serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Smashed Cucumber Salad is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one side-dish portion as commonly served in Chinese restaurants or home kitchens.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cucumber, with peel, raw (FDC ID 168409) and Salad dressing, sesame seed dressing (FDC ID 172539); composite estimate for smashed cucumber salad with soy-sesame dressing
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Smashed Cucumber Salad (Pai Huang Gua) as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for raw cucumber (FDC ID 168409) combined with a soy-sesame-vinegar dressing component. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce contribution. Cholesterol value of 26 mg/100g from the input record is flagged as anomalous for a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; this value is retained from the input HPB-attributed source but noted as requiring verification. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with USDA cucumber and sesame oil data. Input record source (MyHealth Ministry of Health Malaysia) is not on the approved source list; USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved reference anchor.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cucumber and Low-Carbohydrate Vegetable Dishes — analogous estimate based on cucumber GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Smashed Cucumber Salad (Pai Huang Gua) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with raw cucumber (GI approximately 15, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant ingredient. The addition of vinegar (Chinkiang black vinegar) is consistent with published evidence that acetic acid further attenuates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (15 × 8.25) / 100 ≈ 1.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce (380 mg/100g; approximately 570 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as a standard 150 g serving contributes approximately 570 mg sodium, representing roughly 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ≈ 15) and low glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 1.2), with vinegar further attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Smashed Cucumber Salad is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI and minimal carbohydrate content (5.5 g/100g), and the Chinkiang black vinegar in the dressing may additionally blunt postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from soy sauce is a concern for cardiovascular risk; sesame oil provides beneficial unsaturated fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the dish's sesame oil provides heart-healthy unsaturated fats, but the high sodium content (380 mg/100g) warrants portion control or low-sodium soy sauce substitution to reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cucumber and garlic contain antioxidants and phytochemicals with potential anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The antioxidant compounds in cucumber and garlic may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management; the dish is low in pro-inflammatory saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Smashed Cucumber Salad, known in Mandarin as Pāi Huáng Guā (拍黄瓜), is a classic Chinese cold appetizer originating from Sichuan and Northern Chinese culinary traditions. The preparation technique involves smashing whole cucumbers with the flat side of a cleaver or a rolling pin, which fractures the flesh into irregular pieces with rough surfaces that absorb the dressing far more effectively than knife-cut slices. The dressing typically combines minced garlic, light soy sauce, Chinkiang black vinegar (镇江香醋), toasted sesame oil, and chili oil, producing a bold flavour profile that is simultaneously savoury, tangy, nutty, and mildly spicy. The texture is refreshingly crunchy with a slight yielding quality from the smashing. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (approximately 65 kcal/100g) and carbohydrates (5.5 g/100g), with a very low glycemic index of approximately 15, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The primary nutritional concern is sodium (380 mg/100g) contributed by soy sauce, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Sesame oil contributes beneficial unsaturated fatty acids. The dish is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and gluten-free when tamari is substituted for soy sauce. It is commonly served as a starter, palate cleanser, or cooling side dish alongside rich or spicy main courses in Chinese cuisine.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Smashed Cucumber Salad as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for raw cucumber and sesame-soy dressing components. The input record's original source (MyHealth Ministry of Health Malaysia) is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved reference anchor. Core macros are plausible and internally consistent. Cholesterol value from the input (26 mg/100g) has been corrected to 0.0 mg/100g as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; the original value is documented in notes.
confidence_reason_detail: Medium confidence assigned because: (1) no exact approved-source match for this composite dish exists; (2) values are estimated from component-level USDA data; (3) one input value (cholesterol) required correction.
notes: 1. CHOLESTEROL CORRECTION: The input record reported cholesterol_mg = 26.0, attributed to HPB. This value is biologically implausible for a plant-based dish (cucumber, garlic, vinegar, sesame oil, chili oil) containing no animal-derived ingredients. Cholesterol has been set to 0.0 mg/100g (confirmed zero — plant-based dish with no cholesterol-containing ingredients per USDA FoodData Central). The original input value is flagged for upstream review. 2. SOURCE SUBSTITUTION: The input record cited 'MyHealth Ministry of Health Malaysia' (http://www.myhealth.gov.my) as the nutrition source. This source is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. USDA FoodData Central has been used as the approved reference anchor (cucumber FDC ID 168409; sesame oil FDC ID 171016). 3. VITAMIN B12 AND VITAMIN D: Both are 0.0 — confirmed zero, as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal products or fortification. 4. WATER_G: Set to -1 (data not available); detailed moisture content for this composite dish preparation is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for the assembled dish. 5. SATURATED FAT, MUFA, PUFA: Estimated from USDA sesame oil and cucumber data; saturated_fat_g = 0.6, monounsaturated_fat_g = 1.7, polyunsaturated_fat_g = 2.0 — these are approximate values consistent with the fat composition of sesame oil as the primary fat source. 6. TRANS FAT: Set to 0.0 — confirmed zero; no hydrogenated fats in the ingredient list. 7. PORTIONS: The input record contained two identical duplicate portion entries (both '1 serving (150g)', 150g). Only one unique portion is retained in the output. 8. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact code exists for smashed cucumber salad as a prepared Chinese dish. Codes assigned are the closest defensible anchors without fabrication. 9. GLYCEMIC LOAD CALCULATION: GL per 150g serving = (15 × 5.5 × 1.5) / 100 = (15 × 8.25) / 100 ≈ 1.2 (rounded to one decimal place). 10. HALAL STATUS: Marked halal=true as all ingredients (cucumber, garlic, vinegar, sesame oil, chili oil, soy sauce) are plant-derived; however, consumers should verify specific brand compliance for soy sauce and chili oil.
